channel.title: Comments for page "Archival Document -- HSA-008-Advent"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Archival Document -- HSA-008-Advent"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed this article. Despite the length, it never gets boring and has a nice amount of different things to keep you interested.
I particularly thought the action was very entertaining. It incorporates so many different things and abilities, which just makes it a really fun to read, and I felt the use of the agents' perspectives was quite nicely done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for Special Operative Josef.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your link didn't seem to work, so I'm putting it here for anyone interested: https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Afrika_Korps

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Obscure in English mainly means unknown or hidden, but the mystic and sinister meanings are there too.

Will note, though, that the trappings like the skull logo and black uniform were part of an intentional effort to cultivate an image of fear and brutality- they wanted to come across as the scary bad guys, because it was useful for intimidation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Obskura" sounds Latin. In German the adjective is "obskur", the noun is "Obskurität". Though its meaning as occult and dark mystic is not wrong it also means shady, treacherous and such. I think "obscure" in English only means occult and dark mystic etc, right?
I think it perfectly fits in English. I mean, everybody knows from the name alone that it probably is something occult and paramilitary.
But at least to me it sounds weird in German. Naming a black-mages-GOI something occult is fitting, but calling an SS unit something related to criminals? Also the latinish "a" after Obskur doesn't want to fit the Nazi-theme for me.
Especially the SS favored German or pseudo-germanic terms. Some completely made up, sometimes names of VIP, e.g. the units leader, or locations. The OK's director was this Dr. Weiss, right? So it could have been named "Gruppe Weiss" or "Korps Weiss" if you want to use "Korps". Also some other projects where codenamed. A defensive bio-weapon research group was called "Blitzableiter" (lightning-rod) as it started with "b". So any German noun starting with "o" would go as a codename for something occult.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, and "Obskura" wouldn't fit, why?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well I think the Totenkopf of the SS originated from the skulls that some elite soldiers from the WWI storm battalions and some pioneers wore. I think it is rather a symbol of elitism and self-sacrifice than of villainy. According to the official description of the Totenkopfringe it stands for "the reminder to at all times be ready to give the life of our self for the life of the entirety".
It was also already used before WWI, by units that thereby told their foe they would not take prisoners, nor be taken. Also British and US forces have used skulls in their heraldy and traditions too, and some US forces are still using it. I don't think it is to be considered a symbol of villainy here either.
The term "Blutfahne" origins from the Holy Roman Empire and symbolized the Emperors high justice. "Sonnenrad" is nothing extraordinary, just another symbol.
I think all these and other symbols as well as the black SS uniforms, use of runes etc. were meant to create a mysticism to attract recruits and strengthen the bond of the units. The principle is normal in military, in the past and present. All military units have their symbols, their rituals, traditions and such. The Nazis knew very well how to use these to control the masses. You can see similar heavy use of symbols in many communist states and fascist dictatorships as well as the Roman Empire.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: IMO it's a bit pulpy, but I don't particularly have a problem with that, and it isn't wholly implausible from an organization that had the Totenkopfring, the Blutfahne, and the Sonnenrad. Real Nazis did often dress and play act like operatic villains.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, right, totally forgot about those and there are contradicting informations about that term on Wikipedia…

The Obskurakorps' name is too old to do anything about it anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
although the term was not used in the Wehrmacht as far as I know.

Huh? There's the one super famous German expeditionsry corps. But there were hundreds of corps level organizations, here's a list.

Not to mention that the term does not sound like anything the SS would come up with

The SS also had corps (see the same list). As far as "obskura" goes, I don't think that it is that out of line with other occult sounding words in (mis-) use within the Ahnenerbe (and Nazi mysticism in general, e.g. "santur").

The main conceit here is that an Ahnenerbe element was organized as a corps, which yes is total historical nonsense, but so is the whole secret wizard war to begin with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First things first, corps is written Korps in German so unless "Obskuracorps" is a translation it should be written "Obskurakorps". Not to mention that the term does not sound like anything the SS would come up with and a korps is a military unit the size of 3 divisions, although the term was not used in the Wehrmacht as far as I know.

I agree with Nederbörd, despite the setting it has little to do with Nazis, it's more like comic-good-magicians vs. comic-villain-magicians, and magic energy summed up to an equivalent of a modern medium sized nuke. The ease with witch all of them use their powers feels odd to me. They all seem way too powerfull.

I came here and read this series of articles to see if the OK is compatible with the SKP, a Nazi GOI of the German branch. It is not apparently. Furthermore it seems the OK is exactly what I wanted to prevent when I created the SKP hastingly shortly after the german branch was founded. As mentioned by others in the discussion of the Black Rope tale, hollywood Nazis contradict what we find appropriate use of the Nazi topic.

However I do not want to badmouth your article, it is well written and the format fits the AOI I think.

Btw. why does nobody try to shoot the Golem in the head? I'd think they are well prepared and know what they are doing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The history geek in me adores this series so far.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neat. This is a GOC GoI format I can get behind.

I think your combination of the event timeline with the testimony of the agents was a good decision, as it gave a good deal of life and imagery to what otherwise might have been pure exposition. Additionally, the voices of the different operatives really come through, leaving you with a good cast of characters.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the image of the facility priests just chucking every artifact they could find at the AOI team. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don’t actually have a set authorcanon on the dwarves, but I’m leaning towards the mythological depictions in Germanic and Norse mythos. May write about it more if I get any ideas.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was not expecting the Golem to get a voice! :D This was a great payoff to the Obskurakorps memos.

I do have one question: dwarves? Like, in the sense of Norse depictions thereof, or just little people who were apparently very good at creating magic stuff?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough. With regards to the practically throwing artifacts stuff, that's more about how the priests became desperate enough to use whatever to stop the intrusion, even if it was valuable. AOI combat teams are a force to be reckoned with, especially ones with Josef on them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the detail that went into researching the background and writing the descrption and reconnaissance. I leays appreciate good research.

I really do not like the very Hollywood-esque narrative style of the reports and testimonies, however. They seem very much to follow the the pattern of "awesome fights with magic doomsday Nazis" formula that's so emblematic of American comic books and cinema. Which, quite frankly, I feel only serves to dumb them down into cartoonish villains. The embellishments of the overly detailed fights strikes me as unprofessional, and the reckless flinging and destruction of the artifacts and magical items, which the Obskurakorps likely spent years no small fortune acquiring, just seems plain stupid. It's like teying to keep your enemy at bay by throwing ammunition at them.

Sorry if I'm harsh, but I've heard quite a few things about how clichéed Obskura articles are, and now that I've finally seen it, I'm… I dunno, disheartened? Is that the right word?

As such, I feel I will have to give this a -1, in spite of all the good effort that went into writing this. I'm sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeessssssss finally this is posted so I can give it the +1 it deserves for that super incredible fight scene and description.

item.title: Wolfenstein: The More Foundationversey Order
item.content:encoded:
Team "A Weapon to Surpass Metal Gear" is coming in hot and is ready to punch Nazis.

Thanks to OthellotheCat, Taffeta, fieldstone, A Random Day, Captain Kirby, tawny, PeppersGhost for reading and critting. Special thanks to my teammates GreenWolf, ChaoSera, and sirpudding for their feedback and brainstorming help.

For the record, although this isn't directly from the GOC, the format and group it originates from is similar enough to the GOC that I am considering this a GOC GoI format.

Image sources:

  • https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:King_Svafrlame_Secures_the_Sword_Tyrfing.jpg
  • https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Jarlshof_20080821_-_wheelhouse.jpg
  • https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Vemork_Hydroelectric_Plant_1935.jpg
